rationale.R1.1: Section explicitly asserts two 'load-bearing contributions' — the copy-on-write multi-version index and the BALD-style scheduler — creating an internal tension in the framing that a review must interrogate; the gap vs. Delta Lake and LSM-tree literature also requires scrutiny.
rationale.R1.2: Multiple technical causal chains require consistency checking: the O(1) amortized random-read claim, the size-tiered-over-leveled compaction argument, the O(log N) read-amplification assertion, and the claim that without per-annotator indexing the scheduler becomes intractable. Failure to verify any of these invalidates the design rationale.
rationale.R1.3: The 38% annotator-hours reduction claim is quantitative and deferred to Section 5; a review must assess whether that forward reference adequately supports the claim made here, and whether the O(1)/O(log N) complexity claims are derivation-backed or asserted.
rationale.R1.4: The section positions LACUNA explicitly against Delta Lake (row-granularity vs. cell-trajectory versioning) and Cassandra/RocksDB (size-tiered vs. leveled compaction); the novelty delta on each axis is central and must be evaluated.
rationale.R1.7: O'Neil et al., Steinhardt et al., and Houlsby et al. are invoked by name but without full citations; Delta Lake is referenced without attribution. A review should verify that prior-work characterizations are accurate and that omitted references (e.g., other active-learning acquisition frameworks) are not material gaps.

parsed.clarification_question: Should the review focus on technical correctness and contribution framing (logical consistency, evidence-claim alignment, novelty delta vs. cited prior work), or primarily on writing quality and section structure (prose clarity, paragraph organization, narrative flow)?
